one_line_takeaway: Hiding functionality-preserving adversarial perturbations in a freshly-carved cave WITHIN a PE section (restored at runtime by a 24-byte code loader) evades MalConv/MalConv2 at up to ~92-98% vs single-digit-to-40% for append attacks - but only under white-box access, disabled ASLR, 32-bit x86, and files with a large enough section.
key_finding: Intra-section injection dramatically outperforms the append baseline. With gradient descent and 15% perturbation, the best-section evasion reached 92.31% (.rdata) vs 16.17% append against MalConv, and 97.93% (.text) vs 4.01% append against MalConv2. With FGSM the maxima were 96.26% (.rdata) vs 40.92% append against MalConv, and 94.34% (.rdata) vs 54.75% append against MalConv2. A striking, model-dependent effect: the best section for MalConv is .rdata while .text is worst, but for MalConv2 the ordering flips (.text best, .rdata worst) - the authors attribute this to differing per-section attention, supported by convolutional-activation plots (Fig. 8). The code loader plus VM/debugger testing supports the claim that functionality/executability is preserved.
core_contribution: The paper proposes 'intra-section code cave injection', a new location/technique for functionality-preserving adversarial evasion attacks on Windows PE malware. Rather than appending perturbation bytes to the end of the file (the common baseline) or inserting caves BETWEEN sections (Yuste et al.'s inter-section approach, which the authors argue is easily red-flagged), the authors carve an empty 'code cave' INSIDE an existing section (.text, .data, or .rdata). To do this they enlarge the last section, relocate the displaced original bytes there, and place the perturbation in the freed intra-section space. Their second, tightly-coupled contribution is a small (24-byte, x86) code loader injected into the last 24 bytes of the .text section: the PE entry point (OEP) is redirected to it so that at runtime it copies the original bytes back over the cave/perturbation and jumps to the real OEP, thereby restoring the malware's original behavior and executability. Note the two adversarial-optimization routines themselves are NOT the paper's contribution: gradient descent is adopted from Kolosnjaji et al. and FGSM from Goodfellow/Kreuk; the novelty is the injection location plus the restoration mechanism.
threat_model: White-box adversarial EVASION attack (test-time, problem-space) against static ML malware detectors. Attack surface = the malware test sample itself, which the adversary can modify byte-for-byte and inject into. Adversary goal: cause a trained detector to misclassify malware as benign (evasion is defined as the detector's malicious confidence dropping below 50%) WHILE preserving the malware's runtime functionality and executability. Adversary knowledge: full white-box access to the target model, using its gradients to craft perturbations. Targets are the CNN raw-byte detectors MalConv (2MB input cap) and MalConv2 (16MB cap). The authors explicitly state they did NOT test black-box settings.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) white-box only, black-box untested; (2) ASLR/base relocation must be disabled, an observable and unrealistic modification; (3) the code loader is hardcoded x86 and only attacks 32-bit PEs; (4) the attack only applies to malware that has at least one section >=15% of file size - only 150/1000, 260/1000, and 145/1000 files qualified for .text/.data/.rdata respectively; packed malware and obfuscated-section-name malware were excluded; (5) functionality verification only confirms error-free execution, not full behavioral equivalence. Reviewer-noted caveats: the ONLY experimental baseline is the append attack - the richer comparison to nine prior works (Table I) is purely qualitative, not reproduced; the authors' own append-attack evasion (16.17%) conflicts with Kolosnjaji et al.'s reported ~60%, attributed to using 3 iterations vs 20, so the baseline may be weak; gradient-descent attacks required initializing the cave with the target section's original bytes (zero-init evaded nothing), an unexplained dependency; .text FGSM underperformance is acknowledged as unexplained.

datasets_used: 6000 Windows 32-bit PE malware binaries from a VirusTotal public academic repository, across 8 families: Trojan (1600), Dropper (947), Downloader (897), Worm (435), Adware (382), Virus (373), Ransomware (261), Spyware (171), others (934)
1000 Windows 32-bit benign PE files from Kaggle (DikeDataset)
Attack subsets from 1000 malware: 150 eligible for .text, 260 for .data, 145 for .rdata, 500 for the append baseline
quantitative_results: Detector accuracy on authors' data: MalConv 94%, MalConv2 98%
Gradient descent @15% perturbation vs MalConv: append 16.17%, .text 63.45%, .data 69.77%, .rdata 92.31% evasion
Gradient descent @15% perturbation vs MalConv2: append 4.01%, .text 97.93%, .data 54.76%, .rdata 16.67% evasion
FGSM max evasion vs MalConv: append 40.92%, .text 18.90%, .data 63.60%, .rdata 96.26% (best epsilon=0.5)
FGSM max evasion vs MalConv2: append 54.75%, .text 73.86%, .data 77.69%, .rdata 94.34% (best epsilon=1.0)
.rdata evasion vs MalConv reached 49.36% with only 1% perturbation (gradient descent)
Confidence reduction: vs MalConv, >46% of .rdata adversarial files got >=80% confidence drop; vs MalConv2, ~14% of .text/.data files got >=80% drop; append performed worst on this metric too
Code loader is 24 bytes; perturbation capped at 15% of file size
baselines_compared: Append attack (perturbation added at end of file, in the style of Kolosnjaji et al. [5]) - the only experimentally reproduced baseline, run for both gradient descent and FGSM

Qualitative-only comparison (Table I) against nine prior works: Anderson et al., Hu et al., Rosenberg et al., Kreuk et al., Kolosnjaji et al., Suciu et al., Khormali et al., Demetrio et al. (x2), and Yuste et al. (inter-section code caves) - contrasted on target model, knowledge, perturbation/attack location, and functionality preservation, but NOT re-run experimentally
